title: C# Advanced — Paper 3
description: Advanced C# covering LINQ Core & Advanced Operators, Lambda & Functional Patterns, Delegates/Func/Action/Predicate, Events & Event Patterns, Span<T>/Memory<T>, Reflection, Attributes, Extension Methods & Fluent APIs, and Expression Trees.

questions.text: What is the purpose of the LINQ 'let' clause in query syntax?

options.text: It terminates a query and materializes results.
It introduces a new range variable that stores the result of a sub-expression for reuse within the query.
It declares a local variable outside the query context.
It acts as an alias for the 'select' clause.

questions.text: Which LINQ method returns the first element of a sequence, throwing an exception if empty, versus returning a default value if empty?

options.text: First() throws InvalidOperationException if empty; Single() returns default.
First() throws InvalidOperationException if empty; FirstOrDefault() returns default(T) if empty.
First() returns null if empty; FirstOrDefault() throws an exception.
Both First() and FirstOrDefault() throw an exception if the sequence is empty.

questions.text: What does the Aggregate overload with a seed value do in LINQ?

options.text: It starts accumulation from the seed value instead of the first element, applying the accumulator to each subsequent element.
It groups elements starting from a specific index.
It sets the initial size of the result collection.
It specifies the default value if the sequence is empty.

questions.text: What problem can arise when capturing a loop variable inside a lambda that runs after the loop?

options.text: The lambda cannot be compiled because loop variables are not in scope.
All lambdas capture the same variable reference, so when executed after the loop, they all see the final loop value rather than the value at capture time.
The lambda creates a defensive copy of the loop variable automatically.
The loop variable is treated as a const inside the lambda.

questions.text: How does partial application differ from currying in C# functional patterns?

options.text: Partial application and currying are the same concept.
Partial application fixes some arguments of a function returning a new function for the remaining arguments; currying transforms an n-argument function into n chained single-argument functions.
Currying fixes one argument; partial application requires fixing all arguments.
Partial application applies a function to a collection; currying applies it to a single value.

questions.text: What is an anonymous method in C# and how does it differ from a lambda?

options.text: Anonymous methods use the 'delegate' keyword and cannot capture variables; lambdas can.

An anonymous method is declared with the 'delegate' keyword and a block body; a lambda uses '=>' syntax. Both can capture variables, but lambdas also support expression bodies and can be converted to expression trees.
Anonymous methods are faster than lambdas at runtime.
Lambdas are only usable with LINQ; anonymous methods work everywhere.

questions.text: What is the standard event pattern in C# and what is the signature of EventHandler<TEventArgs>?

options.text: EventHandler<TEventArgs> has signature: void Handler(TEventArgs e).
EventHandler<TEventArgs> has signature: void Handler(object sender, TEventArgs e), following the standard pattern where sender is the event source and TEventArgs derives from EventArgs.
EventHandler<TEventArgs> returns TEventArgs and takes no parameters.
EventHandler<TEventArgs> is a generic class, not a delegate.

questions.text: How do you create a custom EventArgs class to pass data with an event?

options.text: Inherit from Delegate and add properties for the event data.
Inherit from EventArgs and add properties for the data; use EventHandler<YourEventArgs> as the event's delegate type.
Implement IEventArgs interface with the required properties.
Pass a Dictionary<string,object> directly to the EventHandler.

questions.text: What is a Memory<T> slice and how is it created from an existing Memory<T>?

options.text: Memory<T> does not support slicing.
A Memory<T> slice is a view over a sub-range, created via memory.Slice(start) or memory.Slice(start, length), pointing to the same underlying data without copying.
Slicing a Memory<T> creates a new array with copied elements.
You create a Memory<T> slice using Array.Copy()

questions.text: What is the performance advantage of using typeof(T).GetProperty().GetValue() vs a compiled expression for repeated property reads via reflection?

options.text: Reflection GetValue is always faster due to JIT caching.

Compiling an Expression<Func<T,TProp>> into a delegate is significantly faster for repeated calls because it avoids per-call reflection overhead, while raw reflection involves boxing and metadata lookup each time.
Both have identical performance.
Dynamic keyword is always faster than both.

questions.text: What does [Conditional("DEBUG")] attribute do when applied to a method?

options.text: It makes the method run only on debug builds of the runtime.
It causes the compiler to omit all call sites to that method when the specified symbol (e.g. DEBUG) is not defined, effectively making calls no-ops without removing the method itself.
It adds a breakpoint to every call of the method.
It converts the method to an inline call.

questions.text: What is an Expression Tree in C# and how does it differ from a compiled lambda?

options.text: An expression tree and a compiled lambda are identical at runtime.
An expression tree (Expression<Func<T>>) represents code as data (a tree of nodes) that can be inspected and translated (e.g., to SQL); a compiled lambda is executable IL code.
Expression trees are used only for event handling.
Expression trees can only represent boolean predicates.

questions.text: How do LINQ query providers like Entity Framework use expression trees?

options.text: They compile expression trees into IL and execute them in-process.
They traverse the expression tree to translate it into another language (such as SQL), enabling execution on a remote data source rather than in-memory.
They use expression trees solely for caching query results.
They convert expression trees into delegate chains automatically.

questions.text: What is the Action<> delegate type in C# and how does it differ from Func<>?

options.text: Action<> can return a value; Func<> cannot.
Action<> is a delegate that takes zero or more parameters and returns void; Func<> takes zero or more parameters and always returns a value (the last type parameter is the return type).
Action<> is only used for event handlers; Func<> is for LINQ.
They are identical except for naming conventions.

questions.text: How do you prevent a memory leak when a long-lived object subscribes to an event on a short-lived object?

options.text: Use static events to avoid instance references.
Unsubscribe the event handler (using -=) when the subscriber is done, or use the weak event pattern so the publisher does not prevent the subscriber from being garbage collected.
Mark the subscriber class as sealed to prevent GC issues.
Call GC.Collect() after each event firing.

questions.text: How can extension methods be used to build a fluent API for configuration?

options.text: Extension methods cannot be used in fluent APIs because they are static.
Each extension method takes the configuration object as 'this' parameter, modifies it, and returns it, allowing chained calls like: config.SetHost(...).SetPort(...).EnableTLS().
Fluent APIs require interface default implementations, not extension methods.
Extension methods in fluent APIs must return void.

questions.text: What is the difference between Expression.Lambda() and Expression.Call() in the System.Linq.Expressions API?

options.text: Expression.Lambda() creates a method call node; Expression.Call() creates a lambda node.
Expression.Lambda() creates a LambdaExpression that wraps a body and parameters into a callable delegate shape; Expression.Call() creates a node representing a method call within an expression tree.
They are interchangeable.
Expression.Call() can only call static methods.

questions.text: What does the 'this' modifier do in an extension method parameter, and what class must contain the extension method?

options.text: It makes the parameter optional; any class can contain extension methods.
It designates the parameter as the type being extended, allowing the method to be called as an instance method; the extension method must be in a non-generic, non-nested static class.
It marks the parameter as a ref parameter.
It allows the extension method to access private members of the extended type.

questions.text: In the context of custom event args, why should TEventArgs inherit from EventArgs rather than being a plain class?

options.text: The compiler requires EventArgs inheritance for all event delegates.

It follows the .NET event design guideline, enables compatibility with EventHandler<TEventArgs>, and allows the event args to be extended with additional properties while maintaining a consistent pattern.
EventArgs provides automatic thread-safety for event data.
Only EventArgs subclasses can be passed to event handlers.

questions.text: What does the LINQ Distinct() method use to determine equality between elements?

options.text: It always uses reference equality.
It uses the default equality comparer for the element type (EqualityComparer<T>.Default), or an IEqualityComparer<T> overload if provided.
It uses the == operator only.
It uses GetHashCode() but ignores Equals().

questions.text: What does a ref struct guarantee that a regular struct does not?

options.text: A ref struct is always allocated on the heap.

A ref struct is guaranteed to live only on the stack; it cannot be boxed, stored in arrays, assigned to interfaces, or used as generic type arguments, providing safety guarantees for stack-only lifetime.
A ref struct cannot contain any reference-type fields.
A ref struct is thread-safe by default.

questions.text: How would you use reflection to determine if a type implements a specific interface?

options.text: typeof(MyType).HasInterface(typeof(IMyInterface))
typeof(IMyInterface).IsAssignableFrom(typeof(MyType)) or typeof(MyType).GetInterfaces().Contains(typeof(IMyInterface))
MyType is IMyInterface (this is not reflection)
Assembly.Implements(typeof(MyType), typeof(IMyInterface))

questions.text: How can you build and compile an expression tree that represents the lambda x => x * 2?

options.text: Expression.Lambda(x => x * 2).Compile()

var param = Expression.Parameter(typeof(int), "x"); var body = Expression.Multiply(param, Expression.Constant(2)); var lambda = Expression.Lambda<Func<int,int>>(body, param); var fn = lambda.Compile();
Expression.Create<Func<int,int>>(x => x * 2)
ExpressionBuilder.Build("x * 2").Compile<int>()

questions.text: What is memoization as a functional pattern and how can it be implemented with a dictionary and Func<T,TResult>?

options.text: Memoization is the same as lazy initialization.
Memoization caches the result of a function for a given input; it can be implemented by wrapping a Func<T,TResult> to check a dictionary for a cached result before calling the original function.
Memoization stores intermediate results in a stack.
Memoization is only applicable to void methods.

questions.text: What is the purpose of the [Serializable] attribute in C#?

options.text: It enables JSON serialization via System.Text.Json.
It marks a class so the .NET binary formatter can serialize/deserialize its instances; without it, BinaryFormatter will throw a SerializationException.
It makes all properties of the class auto-serializable to XML.
It is a compiler hint to optimize field layout.

questions.text: In LINQ, what is the difference between IQueryable<T> and IEnumerable<T>?

options.text: IQueryable<T> executes queries in-memory; IEnumerable<T> queries are translated to SQL.
IEnumerable<T> executes queries in-memory using delegates; IQueryable<T> holds an expression tree that can be translated and executed on a remote data source (e.g., database).
They are identical and interchangeable.
IQueryable<T> does not support deferred execution.

questions.text: What does the Delegate.Combine() method do, and how does it relate to the += operator on delegates?

options.text: Delegate.Combine() removes a delegate from the invocation list; += adds one.
Delegate.Combine() merges two delegates into a new multicast delegate with both in its invocation list; the += operator on a delegate is syntactic sugar for Delegate.Combine().
Delegate.Combine() compiles two delegates into one IL method.
Delegate.Combine() is only available for event types, not raw delegates.

questions.text: What does the LINQ Take(n) operator do and what happens when n exceeds the sequence length?

options.text: Take(n) returns exactly n elements, throwing an exception if fewer exist.
Take(n) returns up to n elements from the start of the sequence; if the sequence has fewer than n elements, it returns all available elements without throwing.
Take(n) removes the first n elements from the sequence.
Take(n) returns the last n elements.

questions.text: How does the using declaration (C# 8+) differ from the traditional using statement?

options.text: The using declaration wraps code in a try/finally block manually.
The using declaration (without braces) disposes the resource at the end of the enclosing scope rather than at the end of a block, reducing nesting.
The using declaration does not call Dispose(); it calls Close() instead.
The using declaration is only for async contexts.

questions.text: What is Span<T>.CopyTo() and how does it differ from Array.Copy()?

options.text: Span<T>.CopyTo() requires the same type as the source; Array.Copy() allows type covariance.

Span<T>.CopyTo(destination) copies all elements to the destination Span<T>, working over any contiguous memory including stack-allocated memory; Array.Copy() is limited to managed arrays and performs bounds-checked copying.
They are functionally identical.
Array.Copy() is faster for all scenarios.
